questions.reviewPearl: DID develops to protect the primary self from intense trauma and requires intense psychotherapy with hypnosis to align the personalities.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with recurring "blackouts" where she loses hours of time. Her husband reports she sometimes acts like a completely different, aggressive person. She has a history of severe childhood trauma.
questions.educationalObjective: Identify dissociative identity disorder based on blackouts and paradoxical distinct identity behaviors witnessed by others.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Dissociative identity disorder presents with 2 or more distinct personality states and memory gaps, often following severe prolonged trauma.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Dissociative amnesia involves memory loss of an event but strictly lacks distinct personality states and paradoxical behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: DID develops to protect the primary self from intense trauma and requires intense psychotherapy with hypnosis to align the personalities.

questions.reviewPearl: Fugue is a specific subtype of dissociative amnesia triggered by major stressors that involves travel.
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is found wandering in another state with no memory of his past or identity. He recently survived a horrific house fire.
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize dissociative amnesia with fugue in a patient with stress-induced memory loss and physical travel.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Dissociative amnesia with purposeful travel or bewildered wandering specifically constitutes a dissociative fugue.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Depersonalization involves an out-of-body experience but maintains intact memory and autobiographical self without travel.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Fugue is a specific subtype of dissociative amnesia triggered by major stressors that involves travel.

questions.reviewPearl: Patients with depersonalization often describe their experience as living in a dream, yet their reality testing is completely intact.
questions.stem: A 17-year-old boy reports feeling as though he is watching a movie of himself from the outside. He knows this feeling isn't reality.
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose depersonalization derealization disorder based on out-of-body feelings with strictly intact reality testing.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Depersonalization is characterized by out-of-body experiences while reality testing remains intact, commonly seen in adolescents.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Psychotic disorders like schizophrenia feature impaired reality testing, unlike depersonalization disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Patients with depersonalization often describe their experience as living in a dream, yet their reality testing is completely intact.

questions.reviewPearl:
Dissociative amnesia can involve memory loss isolated solely to the traumatic event, but severe forms can erase the complete autobiographical self.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 2: Somatic Symptom Disorders
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman cannot recall the details of a severe car crash she survived two weeks ago, but remembers everything else about her life.
questions.educationalObjective: Identify dissociative amnesia presenting as isolated memory loss for a traumatic stressor.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Dissociative amnesia involves memory loss of a specific traumatic event without physical travel or altered identity.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Factitious disorder involves intentional symptom production, whereas this is stress-induced subconscious memory loss.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Dissociative amnesia can involve memory loss isolated solely to the traumatic event, but severe forms can erase the complete autobiographical self.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 2: Somatic Symptom Disorders

questions.retrievalTag: IAD excessive illness preoccupation no symptoms
questions.reviewPearl: The anxiety in illness anxiety disorder focuses on potentially acquiring a disease rather than a specific physical pain.
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man fears he has colon cancer. He has absolutely no somatic symptoms, but obsessively seeks reassurance from multiple providers despite negative workups.
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate illness anxiety disorder (no somatic symptoms) from somatic symptom disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Excessive preoccupation with acquiring a serious illness for 6 months without actual somatic symptoms defines illness anxiety disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Somatic symptom disorder strictly requires the presence of a distressing somatic complaint, which is absent here.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: The anxiety in illness anxiety disorder focuses on potentially acquiring a disease rather than a specific physical pain.

questions.reviewPearl: Patients with somatic symptom disorder experience real distress and should have regular follow-ups with a single dedicated provider.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman complains of persistent chronic back pain for 8 months. Extensive workups are negative, but she spends excessive time seeking explanations for her symptoms.
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize somatic symptom disorder driven by subconscious motivation and excessive symptom preoccupation.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Presence of $$ 1 somatic symptom lasting $$ 6 months with disproportionate preoccupation despite no medical cause is somatic symptom disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Factitious disorder involves intentional symptom fabrication to fulfill a sick role, whereas this patient's motivation is subconscious.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Patients with somatic symptom disorder experience real distress and should have regular follow-ups with a single dedicated provider.

questions.reviewPearl: Treating the disease as its neurologic correlate (e.g., physical rehab) often allows the psyche an escape to return to normal.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with sudden blindness after witnessing a violent crime. Neurological exam is normal and she seems unconcerned about the deficit.
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose conversion disorder in the setting of stress-induced neurological deficits with subconscious motivation.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: A neurologic complaint without organic cause following an emotional stressor, often with "la belle indifference", defines conversion disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Malingering features intentional symptom fabrication for secondary material gain, not an unconscious neurologic deficit.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Treating the disease as its neurologic correlate (e.g., physical rehab) often allows the psyche an escape to return to normal.

questions.reviewPearl:
Factitious disorder involves purposeful deceit without material gain and generally requires confrontation and psychotherapy.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 3: Antipsychotic Medications
questions.stem: A nurse is caught secretly manipulating her lab tests to appear sick. She admits she enjoys the attention of being a patient but denies financial motivation.
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate factitious disorder (sick role motivation) from malingering (material gain motivation).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Intentional symptom fabrication solely to assume the "sick role" (attention) is the hallmark of factitious disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Malingering involves intentional deceit driven by secondary material gain (e.g., insurance money), not simply attention.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Factitious disorder involves purposeful deceit without material gain and generally requires confrontation and psychotherapy.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 3: Antipsychotic Medications

questions.reviewPearl: Treatment for akathisia involves decreasing the antipsychotic dose, or adding beta-blockers or anti-ACh medications like benztropine.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man treated with haloperidol begins pacing the room constantly. He states he has an intense urge to move.
questions.educationalObjective: Identify akathisia as a medication-induced restlessness treatable by decreasing the dose or adding beta-blockers.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Akathisia is an extrapyramidal side effect characterized by a subjective feeling of restlessness and an urge to move.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Acute dystonia involves involuntary muscle contractions (e.g., torticollis), not a subjective feeling of restlessness.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Treatment for akathisia involves decreasing the antipsychotic dose, or adding beta-blockers or anti-ACh medications like benztropine.

questions.reviewPearl: Dopamine antagonism in the tuberoinfundibular tract leads to disinhibition of prolactin, causing hyperprolactinemia and gynecomastia.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with schizophrenia is started on a typical antipsychotic. The medication treats his hallucinations primarily through action on which pathway?
questions.educationalObjective: Understand the mesolimbic pathway is the target for controlling positive psychotic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: First-generation antipsychotics treat positive symptoms by antagonizing D2 receptors in the mesolimbic pathway.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Antagonism in the nigrostriatal pathway causes extrapyramidal side effects, not positive symptom relief.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Dopamine antagonism in the tuberoinfundibular tract leads to disinhibition of prolactin, causing hyperprolactinemia and gynecomastia.

questions.reviewPearl: Clozapine is the best and most effective antipsychotic but remains a drug of last resort due to lethal agranulocytosis.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with refractory schizophrenia is started on clozapine after failing all other medications. What monitoring is legally required?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize agranulocytosis risk and the need for weekly CBC monitoring when prescribing clozapine.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Clozapine carries a life-threatening risk of agranulocytosis, explicitly requiring weekly complete blood counts.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While atypical antipsychotics can prolong QTc, weekly ECGs are not the federally required monitoring standard for clozapine.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Clozapine is the best and most effective antipsychotic but remains a drug of last resort due to lethal agranulocytosis.

questions.reviewPearl:
Stopping the offending drug is the only management for tardive dyskinesia, though symptoms will initially worsen upon cessation.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 4: Personality Disorders
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman on long-term typical antipsychotics develops continuous, involuntary lip-smacking movements.
questions.educationalObjective: Identify dopamine receptor hypersensitization as the core mechanism behind late-onset tardive dyskinesia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Tardive dyskinesia is an irreversible condition caused by dopamine receptor hypersensitization leading to suppressible oral-facial movements.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Dopamine depletion leads to parkinsonism (bradykinesia), not the hyperkinetic oral-facial movements of tardive dyskinesia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Stopping the offending drug is the only management for tardive dyskinesia, though symptoms will initially worsen upon cessation.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 4: Personality Disorders

questions.reviewPearl: Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) is heavily test-associated as the specific treatment of choice for borderline personality disorder.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with superficial wrist cuts. She has unstable relationships and rapidly shifts between idealizing and despising her therapist.
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose borderline personality disorder based on self-harm gestures, mood instability, and splitting.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Borderline PD features impulsivity, suicidal gestures, emotional numbness, and splitting.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Histrionic PD patients are theatrical and hypersexual but lack the severe emotional emptiness, self-harm, and splitting seen here.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) is heavily test-associated as the specific treatment of choice for borderline personality disorder.

questions.reviewPearl: Antisocial personality disorder is the only personality disorder that absolutely cannot be diagnosed prior to 18 years of age.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old man is arrested for fraud. He lacks remorse, acts impulsively, and has a confirmed history of conduct disorder prior to age 18.
questions.educationalObjective: Identify antisocial personality disorder using the criteria of adult criminality and prior childhood conduct disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Antisocial PD is characterized by criminality, impulsivity, lack of remorse, and a prerequisite history of conduct disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Narcissistic PD patients demand special treatment but do not inherently show criminal disregard for others' rights.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Antisocial personality disorder is the only personality disorder that absolutely cannot be diagnosed prior to 18 years of age.

questions.retrievalTag: Avoidant PD fears rejection desires friends
questions.reviewPearl: When interacting with avoidant personality disorder patients, physicians should avoid power struggles and make the patients choose.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old man desires friends but avoids them out of intense fear of rejection. He turns down promotions to avoid criticism.
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate avoidant personality disorder (desires relationships, fears rejection) from schizoid personality disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Avoidant PD involves extreme shyness, fear of criticism, and avoidance of interaction despite desperately wanting relationships.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Schizoid PD patients also isolate themselves but are completely content being alone and desire absolutely no relationships.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: When interacting with avoidant personality disorder patients, physicians should avoid power struggles and make the patients choose.

questions.retrievalTag: Dependent PD submissive fear of alone
questions.reviewPearl:
Patients with dependent PD will generally obey a stronger voice and may even self-sabotage treatment if it threatens their dependent relationship.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 5: Schizophrenia and Other Psychotic Disorders
questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman stays in an abusive relationship because she fears isolation. She submissively relies on others to make decisions.
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize dependent personality disorder presenting as profound submissiveness and reliance on others.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Dependent PD is defined by submissive, clingy behavior and a need to be taken care of due to unrealistic fears of isolation.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Borderline PD features volatile instability rather than the profound, passive submissiveness seen in dependent patients.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Patients with dependent PD will generally obey a stronger voice and may even self-sabotage treatment if it threatens their dependent relationship.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 5: Schizophrenia and Other Psychotic Disorders

questions.reviewPearl: Post-partum psychosis is considered a variant of brief psychotic disorder that occurs directly in response to pregnancy.
questions.stem: A 20-year-old develops auditory hallucinations following college finals. The symptoms last for exactly 2 weeks and then fully resolve.
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose brief psychotic disorder based on the strict timeline of symptom duration (< 1 month).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Psychotic symptoms lasting greater than 1 day but less than 1 month following a stressor is strictly diagnostic of brief psychotic disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Schizophreniform disorder requires psychotic symptoms lasting between 1 and 6 months.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Post-partum psychosis is considered a variant of brief psychotic disorder that occurs directly in response to pregnancy.

questions.reviewPearl: Treatment for schizophreniform disorder generally lasts 3-6 months until the symptoms ideally remit.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents with disorganized speech and delusions for exactly 3 months. He has never had a mood episode.
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate schizophreniform disorder from schizophrenia purely based on the 1-to-6 month symptom timeframe.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Psychosis lasting between 1 month and 6 months fits the exact time criteria for schizophreniform disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Schizophrenia specifically requires symptoms persisting for greater than 6 months.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Treatment for schizophreniform disorder generally lasts 3-6 months until the symptoms ideally remit.

questions.reviewPearl: Differentiating schizoaffective from a mood disorder with psychotic features relies on finding isolated psychosis when mood symptoms are entirely absent.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman has ongoing hallucinations for 6 months. She also had a distinct 3-week manic episode, but psychosis persists when her mood is normal.

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Identify schizoaffective disorder by recognizing persistent psychosis independent of mood episode exacerbations.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Schizoaffective disorder features a primary psychotic disorder (psychosis without mood changes) with superimposed distinct mood episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: In mood disorders with psychotic features, the psychosis is only present when the active mood symptoms predominate.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Differentiating schizoaffective from a mood disorder with psychotic features relies on finding isolated psychosis when mood symptoms are entirely absent.

questions.reviewPearl:
Treatment for delusional disorder requires gentle confrontation over years of psychotherapy, as drugs generally will not work.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 6: Catatonia
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man functions normally at work but holds a fixed, non-bizarre belief that his neighbor is an undercover spy.
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose delusional disorder in a high-functioning patient with isolated, non-bizarre delusions.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Delusional disorder is defined by fixed, non-bizarre delusions without other severe psychotic symptoms or functional decline.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Paranoid personality disorder involves pervasive distrust but lacks fixed, undeniable delusions.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Treatment for delusional disorder requires gentle confrontation over years of psychotherapy, as drugs generally will not work.
eftab720
tightenfactor0
eftab720
tightenfactor0
Document 6: Catatonia

questions.reviewPearl: Catatonic patients are at high risk for DVT and rhabdomyolysis due to prolonged immobility and poor nutritional intake.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with bipolar disorder sits completely motionless. If his arm is raised, he holds the awkward posture (waxy flexibility). Best initial treatment?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize catatonia (mutism, catalepsy, waxy flexibility) and identify lorazepam as the first-line treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Lorazepam is the treatment of choice for catatonia, acting as both a diagnostic and therapeutic agent.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic that can precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome and worsen catatonic rigidity.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Catatonic patients are at high risk for DVT and rhabdomyolysis due to prolonged immobility and poor nutritional intake.

questions.reviewPearl: Lead-pipe rigidity (generalized stiffness and bradyreflexia) is the key physical finding distinguishing NMS from serotonin syndrome.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man on haloperidol develops delirium, autonomic instability, high fever, and extreme "lead-pipe" rigidity.
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate NMS from other hyperthermic emergencies by the use of antipsychotics and lead-pipe rigidity.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: NMS is an antipsychotic-induced emergency presenting with fever, autonomic instability, elevated CK, and lead-pipe rigidity.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Serotonin syndrome features hyperreflexia and clonus, not lead-pipe rigidity, and is specifically caused by SSRIs.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Lead-pipe rigidity (generalized stiffness and bradyreflexia) is the key physical finding distinguishing NMS from serotonin syndrome.

questions.reviewPearl: Clonus and hyperreflexia strongly differentiate serotonin syndrome from the rigid, bradyreflexic state seen in neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old on fluoxetine develops delirium, autonomic instability, fever, bilateral hyperreflexia, and ankle clonus.
questions.educationalObjective: Identify serotonin syndrome based on hyperreflexia, clonus, and recent SSRI use.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Serotonin syndrome presents with fever, autonomic instability, and distinct hyperreflexia/clonus triggered by serotonergic agents.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Malignant hyperthermia is triggered specifically by inhaled anesthetics, not serotonergic antidepressants.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Clonus and hyperreflexia strongly differentiate serotonin syndrome from the rigid, bradyreflexic state seen in neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

questions.reviewPearl: Malignant hyperthermia is treated with dantrolene and classically lacks a psychiatric context, though family history may be present.
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man receives inhaled halothane for surgery. He rapidly develops autonomic instability, high fever, and elevated CK.
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize malignant hyperthermia as a severe hyperthermic and rigid reaction to specific anesthetic gases.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Malignant hyperthermia is a hypermetabolic reaction characterized by rapid fever and rigidity following exposure to inhaled anesthetics.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Malignant catatonia occurs in the context of untreated severe psychiatric illness, lacking distinct drug or anesthesia triggers.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Malignant hyperthermia is treated with dantrolene and classically lacks a psychiatric context, though family history may be present.
